title: Healthy disagreement
html:
When a room agrees with you completely, it is worth pausing before you take it as approval. A 100% ratio of agreement could show that either the idea receivers don’t think for themselves, don’t pay attention, or worst, don’t dare to speak on themselves. Unbroken consensus often signals absence — of friction, of independent minds chewing on the same problem — rather than the presence of a genuinely good idea.

The opposite extreme is no better. A too low ratio of agreement could show that there are too much unknown variables, unaligned background, or a toxic debating culture. When almost no one can meet you, the problem may not be your idea at all; it may be that people are arguing past each other from different maps, or that disagreeing has quietly become a way to score points rather than to find the truth.

So neither pole is the goal. Picture a design review where every reviewer nods and signs off in minutes: it feels efficient, but more often it means the work was never truly examined. There is no such thing as a right ratio of agreement — it shifts with the team, the topic, and the stakes. However, the point here is that a complete agreement is not necessarily a good sign. Healthy disagreement is the texture of people actually thinking, and a little of it is something to welcome rather than smooth away. (A thread I picked up from a friend’s notes.)
